questions.question_text: What is a neural network primarily inspired by?
options: Electrical circuits
The human brain
Decision trees
Statistical regression
questions.explanation: Neural networks are computational models loosely inspired by the structure and function of the human brain, specifically by the way biological neurons communicate.

questions.question_text: What does the loss function measure?
options: The number of neurons in the network
How wrong the network's prediction is
The learning rate of the optimizer
The size of the training dataset
questions.explanation: The loss function quantifies the difference between the network's prediction and the true label. Minimising it is the goal of training.

questions.question_text: Which mathematical principle does backpropagation rely on to compute gradients?
options: The product rule only
Fourier transforms
The chain rule of calculus
Taylor series approximation
questions.explanation: Backpropagation applies the chain rule of calculus to efficiently compute the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight layer by layer.

questions.question_text: Why is ReLU preferred over Sigmoid in deep networks?
options: ReLU outputs values between 0 and 1, making it easier to interpret
ReLU avoids the vanishing gradient problem
ReLU requires fewer parameters than Sigmoid
ReLU is differentiable at all points
questions.explanation: Sigmoid outputs saturate near 0 or 1, causing gradients to become near-zero during backpropagation (vanishing gradient). ReLU does not saturate for positive inputs, keeping gradients healthy in deep networks.

questions.question_text: A model achieves 98% accuracy on training data but only 55% on a held-out test set. Which of the following best describes this situation and its likely remedies? (Select all that apply)
options: The model is overfitting
Applying dropout could help
The model needs more layers to improve
L2 regularisation could help
questions.explanation: A large gap between training and test accuracy is the hallmark of overfitting. Dropout and L2 regularisation are standard remedies. Adding more layers would increase model capacity and likely worsen overfitting.

questions.question_text: Explain why early stopping is an effective regularisation strategy even though it does not modify the network architecture or loss function.
options: It reduces the learning rate exponentially, preventing large weight updates
It prevents the model from training long enough to memorise noise in the training data
It increases the size of the training set by generating synthetic examples
It removes neurons with the smallest weights to simplify the network
questions.explanation: Early stopping halts training when validation loss stops improving. In the early phases of training a model learns genuine patterns; later it begins memorising noise. Stopping before that point implicitly limits model complexity without changing the architecture.
